attributes.stem:
A 76-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with concerns about his increasing fatigue. He reports difficulty keeping up with his usual hobbies such as gardening, noticing that he required an extended period of rest after completing some pruning recently. He has a medical history of hypertension and osteoarthritis. His medications include amlodipine and paracetamol as needed. He lives alone and previously managed all activities of daily living independently.

On examination, he appears slightly underweight with vital signs within normal limits. He has a body mass index of 23 kg/m2 and an unremarkable examination of his neurological, cardiovascular and respiratory system. You decide to perform a frailty assessment.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following physical examination findings would be most concerning for frailty in this patient?
options: Ability to rise from a chair without using arms
Normal range of motion in all joints
attributes.explanation:
In this scenario, the most concerning physical examination finding for frailty would be a gait speed of 0.6 m/s. Gait speed is a well-established and reliable indicator of frailty in older adults. A gait speed below 0.8 m/s is generally considered to be associated with frailty, and 0.6 m/s is particularly concerning as it indicates a this measurement can serve as a direct examination of functional status, given movement's importance in activities of daily living.

A grip strength of 28 kg is slightly reduced for this man's age, but would not be overly concerning for frailty. Grip strength is an important measure of muscle strength and risk of frailty, but this value does not suggest significant weakness.

A body mass index of 23 kg/m2 is within the normal range and would not be a specific indicator of frailty. While both underweight and overweight states can be associated with frailty, this BMI alone is not particularly concerning.

The ability to rise from a chair without using arms is a good sign and suggests preserved lower body strength. Difficulty with this task would be more concerning for frailty, so the ability to perform it is reassuring.

Normal range of motion in all joints is also not specifically indicative of frailty, and reassuringly in this man's case indicates his osteoarthritis is not significantly limiting to his function . While reduced range of motion can be associated with frailty, normal range of motion does not rule it out.

Frailty is a complex syndrome characterized by decreased physiological reserve and increased vulnerability to stressors. There are a variety of validated assessment tools for frailty with some of the major ones incorporating measurement of muscle strength, gait speed, physical activity levels, and sometimes cognitive function. In this case, the slow gait speed is the most objective and concerning finding, as it's a key component of frailty assessments like the Fried Frailty Phenotype and is strongly associated with adverse health outcomes in older adults.

Early identification of frailty is crucial as it allows for timely interventions such as exercise programs, nutritional support, and medication reviews, which can help maintain independence and reduce the risk of falls, hospitalizations, and other adverse outcomes in older adults.
learningPoints: Slow gait speed is a key indicator of frailty in older adults.
Frailty assessment, often with use of validated tools, can include evaluation of muscle strength, gait speed, and physical activity levels
Early identification of frailty can guide interventions to maintain independence and reduce adverse health outcomes in older adults.
references: Castell MV, Sánchez M, Julián R, Queipo R, Martín S, Otero Á. Frailty prevalence and slow walking speed in persons age 65 and older: implications for primary care. BMC Fam Pract. 2013;14:86. Available at: https://bmcprimcare.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/1471-2296-14-86

Massy-Westropp NM, Gill TK, Taylor AW, Bohannon RW, Hill CL. Hand Grip Strength: age and gender stratified normative data in a population-based study. BMC Res Notes. 2011;4:127. Available at: https://bmcresnotes.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/1756-0500-4-127

Navarrete-Villanueva D, Gómez-Cabello A, Marín-Puyalto J, Moreno LA, Vicente-Rodríguez G, Casajús JA. Frailty and Physical Fitness in Elderly People: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. Sports Med. 2021;51(1):143-160. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33201455/

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
